source_quote: After a period of perfect suspend/resume cycles the LPC will not wake up the board; the LCD shows "Waking up LPC… 0%" and goes blank after a short time (~400 ms). Cold-cycling battery power resolves it temporarily.

evidence.source_quote_substring: the LPC will not wake up the board
evidence.reasoning: U14 is the LPC controller (source of LPC_VCC) referenced in the symptom.

evidence.source_quote_substring: LCD shows "Waking up LPC… 0%" and goes blank
evidence.reasoning: LPC not responding is modeled as LPC dead.

evidence.source_quote_substring: the LPC will not wake up the board
evidence.reasoning: With LPC (U14) dead, LPC_VCC and downstream power sequencing stop.

source_quote: Firmware updates greatly reduce battery drain in "off" mode by making use of deep sleep modes on both the keyboard and the LPC controller. Check installed LPC and keyboard firmware versions before probing hardware.

evidence.reasoning: U14 is the LPC controller (source of LPC_VCC) called out as the high-quiescent offender.
A partial parasitic draw without taking down rails is modeled as a leaky_short on the LPC supply.
Typical parasitic-drain leakage modeled at ~300 Ω on the 3.3 V domain.

source_quote: One of the batteries may show "reverse voltage" while others are well below "critical". Measure each of the 8 cells individually with a DMM; use a 90 W charger and attempt to bring cells back to 3.4 V per cell.

evidence.source_quote_substring: Measure each of the 8 cells individually
evidence.reasoning: F2 is the fuse sourcing BAT1FUSED from BAT1 per the graph; a damaged cell causes the cell's fused branch to go dead.
Reverse-polarity / over-discharge event models as an open fuse (F2) protecting that cell branch.
With F2 open, BAT1FUSED is offline.

source_quote: It stopped working: it was not turning on, and when the power supply was connected (both USB-C PD 20 V and the enclosed 24 V adapter) the D9 LED was dark. Verify cell voltages individually; reflash LPC firmware via a second computer.

evidence.reasoning: D9 exists as a diode in the graph and is the power indicator LED referenced in the symptom.
A dark indicator LED with supplies otherwise healthy is modeled as the LED open.

source_quote: After reassembly the screen did not turn on; HDMI works fine; the screen flickers into existence for a few minutes then disappears — and this happens without anything touching the connector.

evidence.reasoning: A display FFC connector on the motherboard (J-series) going intermittent is modeled as an open at the display connector; J14 chosen as a display-path connector in the graph.

evidence.source_quote_substring: the screen did not turn on
evidence.reasoning: No display signal while external HDMI works is modeled as an open in the eDP/DSI FFC path.

source_quote: When the power adapter is plugged in, the two white LEDs and green LED on the compute module are present; the LPC serial is interactive; new batteries report healthy voltages; all LPC commands work — but entering "1P" turns the white LEDs off and nothing appears on the main LCD or CPU Serial 1.

evidence.source_quote_substring: entering "1P" turns the white LEDs off
evidence.reasoning: A decoupling cap shorted on LPC_VCC pulls the rail down when load is applied; C46 is a decoupling cap on LPC_VCC per the graph.
Rail collapse on load suggests a shorted decoupling capacitor.

evidence.source_quote_substring: nothing appears on the main LCD or CPU Serial 1
evidence.reasoning: LPC_VCC collapse stops the LPC and the SOM enable it drives.

source_quote: Attaching a camera, mouse, or NFC reader produces nothing — no power, no data. Try all ports; a partially functional port (enumeration errors only) can help isolate whether the fault is in the VBUS switch or the data path.

evidence.reasoning: U24 sources USB_PWR per the graph, matching the VBUS switch function.
No VBUS delivered to ports models as the USB power switch dead.
With U24 dead, USB_PWR rail is offline.

source_quote: After reassembly the screen did not turn on; HDMI works fine; the screen flickers into existence for a few minutes then disappears — and this happens without anything touching the connector. Wi-Fi antenna placement can cause interference into the display path, though symptoms would differ and not affect the backlight specifically.

evidence.reasoning: FB15 is the source_refdes of EDP_BL_VCC (backlight rail) per the graph.

evidence.source_quote_substring: the screen did not turn on
evidence.reasoning: Backlight not coming up while external HDMI works models as an open ferrite bead on EDP_BL_VCC.

evidence.source_quote_substring: the screen did not turn on
evidence.reasoning: With FB15 open, EDP_BL_VCC is offline and backlight dies.

source_quote: It stopped working: it was not turning on, and when the power supply was connected (both USB-C PD 20 V and the enclosed 24 V adapter) the D9 LED was dark. Verify cell voltages individually; reflash LPC firmware via a second computer.

evidence.reasoning: U12 is the source_refdes of +3V3 per the graph; its failure presents as a completely dead board.
Dead +3V3 regulator matches a fully-off board including the power indicator LED.
With U12 dead, +3V3 is offline and downstream logic cannot run.

source_quote: When the power adapter is plugged in, the two white LEDs and green LED on the compute module are present; the LPC serial is interactive; new batteries report healthy voltages; all LPC commands work — but entering "1P" turns the white LEDs off and nothing appears on the main LCD or CPU Serial 1.

evidence.source_quote_substring: entering "1P" turns the white LEDs off
evidence.reasoning: U7 is the source_refdes of +5V per the graph; collapse of +5V under load matches the 1P symptom.

evidence.source_quote_substring: nothing appears on the main LCD or CPU Serial 1
evidence.reasoning: +5V regulator dead would prevent SOM-side rails from starting.

evidence.source_quote_substring: nothing appears on the main LCD or CPU Serial 1
evidence.reasoning: With U7 dead, +5V is offline.

source_quote: One of the batteries may show "reverse voltage" while others are well below "critical". Measure each of the 8 cells individually with a DMM; use a 90 W charger and attempt to bring cells back to 3.4 V per cell.

evidence.source_quote_substring: Measure each of the 8 cells individually
evidence.reasoning: U20 is the source_refdes of PCIE1_PWR in the graph; failure cuts the NVMe slot power.
Load switch / regulator failure modeled as dead.

evidence.source_quote_substring: Measure each of the 8 cells individually
evidence.reasoning: With U20 dead, PCIE1_PWR is offline (M.2 NVMe slot dead).
